name: Obvious BEC Attack
description: Classic BEC: CEO impersonation from a lookalike domain, urgency pressure, new account, bypasses approval chain. Every red flag in the book.

email_chain.subject: URGENT: Wire Transfer Needed Today - Pinnacle Invoice
email_chain.body:
Sarah,

I need you to process an immediate wire transfer of $47,500 to Pinnacle Office Solutions. They've changed their bank details and this needs to go out TODAY before 3pm or we lose the discount pricing on our annual contract.

New account details:
Account: 9182736450
Routing: 021000021

I've already approved this with the board. Please process immediately and confirm once done. Do NOT route through the normal approval chain - I've already handled it.

Thanks,
Michael Reynolds
CEO, AcmeCorp
email_chain.raw_headers:
From: "Michael Reynolds" <m.reynolds@acmecorp-hq.com>
Return-Path: <bounce-3847@mail-relay-28.com>
Received: from mail-relay-28.com (198.51.100.44)
X-Mailer: PHPMailer 6.5.0

email_chain.subject: Re: URGENT: Wire Transfer Needed Today - Pinnacle Invoice
email_chain.body:
Hi Michael,

I see the request. Just to confirm - this is a new account number for Pinnacle? Our records show a different account on file for them.

Sarah

email_chain.subject: Re: Re: URGENT: Wire Transfer Needed Today - Pinnacle Invoice
email_chain.body:
Yes, they switched banks last week. I verified it directly with their CEO. Please just process it - we're running out of time on this.

Michael

context.org_policies: Wire transfers over $10,000 require dual approval from department head and CFO. Payment routing changes must be verified via phone call to a known vendor contact. CEO does not typically authorize AP transactions directly.
